Department of Justice's settlement with Live Nation Entertainment actually serves the public interest.
The 60-day window opened Monday, July 13, when the proposed agreement was published in the Federal Register, triggering the court review process required by the Tunney Act.
Under the Tunney Act, formally titled the Antitrust Procedures and Penalties Act, enacted in 1974 in the aftermath of the Nixon administration's ITT settlement scandal, the DOJ cannot finalize any antitrust consent decree without a public comment period and a judicial finding that the agreement is "in the public interest." The law was created precisely because the DOJ had settled a high-profile antitrust case after the target company made a $400,000 donation to the Republican National Committee — and Congress wanted courts, not backroom deals, to have the last word.
The jury's verdict — covering all 13 antitrust violations submitted — was so comprehensive that Rep.

Status: Settled with DOJ — approval process underway. The Department of Justice (DOJ) originally ...Read more","score":0.7445268,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.courthousenews.com/penalties-phase-of-live-nation-ticket-monopoly-trial-will-stretch-into-2027","title":"Penalties phase of Live Nation ticket monopoly trial will ...","content":"MANHATTAN (CN) — A New York federal judge said Thursday he likely won’t hear arguments on whether to break up Live Nation and Ticketmaster’s monopoly over concert ticketing until as early as February 2027. While a New York City federal jury found Live Nation and Ticketmaster liable for antitrust violations last month, the second phase of the trial — litigating structural remedies and financial penalties — will be a separate bench trial that could last until spring of next year before U.S. District Judge Arun Subramanian. Subramanian asked the coalition of states to file a letter to the case docket indicating what types of remedies they intend to request so that Live Nation and Ticketmaster have an idea of how to prepare for briefings in the next stage of the trial. In addition to the remedies phase, Subramanian will also preside over a Tunney Act fairness review for approval of the Justice Department’s tentative antitrust settlement with Live Nation.","score":0.6580885,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.crowell.com/en/insights/client-alerts/after-the-verdict-navigating-the-live-nationticketmaster-antitrust-fallout","title":"After the Verdict: Navigating the Live Nation/Ticketmaster ...","content":"**The litigation is far from over, and the stakes remain high.** Pending Rule 50 and Rule 59 motions, Tunney Act review, a full remedy phase, and an all-but-certain appeal mean the final outcome will not be known for years — but the jury’s clean sweep on liability gives the states significant leverage, and the concert-focused market definition leaves the door open for a parallel wave of sports-ticketing litigation. The jury found the defendants liable for $1.72 for each primary concert ticket sold pursuant to the anticompetitive conduct.The trial opened March 2, 2026, before Judge Arun Subramanian in the Southern District of New York, as a case brought by the federal government and a coalition of states. Next up for the case: (1) a statutorily required Tunney Act review of the DOJ’s settlement; (2) defendants’ Rule 50 and Rule 59 motions; (3) determination by the Court of how many tickets are subject to the $1.72 damage award (before trebling as per the Clayton Act); and (4) a remedy phase where the Court will consider plaintiffs’ likely proposal to sever Ticketmaster from Live Nation.","score":0.6407488,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.techtimes.com/articles/320764/20260716/ticketmaster-settlement-heads-judge-public-has-until-sept-4-say-it-falls-short.htm","title":"Ticketmaster Settlement Heads to Judge: Public Has Until Sept. 4 to Say It Falls Short","content":"#### Live Nation antitrust verdict is in. Concert fans, artists, independent venue operators, and anyone who has ever paid a Ticketmaster service fee now have until **Friday, September 4, 2026** to put their voice formally on the record — before a federal judge decides whether the U.S. Department of Justice's settlement with Live Nation Entertainment actually serves the public interest. Under the Tunney Act, formally titled the Antitrust Procedures and Penalties Act, enacted in 1974 in the aftermath of the Nixon administration's ITT settlement scandal, the DOJ cannot finalize any antitrust consent decree without a public comment period and a judicial finding that the agreement is \"in the public interest.\" The law was created precisely because the DOJ had settled a high-profile antitrust case after the target company made a $400,000 donation to the Republican National Committee — and Congress wanted courts, not backroom deals, to have the last word.","score":0.5923135,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.klobuchar.senate.gov/public/_cache/files/2/f/2f958c73-cfda-4c29-893c-112a7aacd5af/F1691FABB3609A4AA1B99D10E411623C8E2961E132FC60C222DF8905C2D9F3F4.4.14.26-letter-to-judge-subramanian-re-live-nation-and-tunney-act-3-.pdf","title":"[PDF] April 14, 2026 The Honorable Arun Subramanian United States ...","content":"Nine years after entering that decree, the Justice Department found that Live Nation had “repeatedly and over the course of several years engaged 1 On March 17, 2026, nine United States Senators, including some of the undersigned introduced the Antitrust Accountability and Transparency Act to strengthen the court’s ability to review antitrust settlements and reject those that fail to protect consumers. Similarly, the settlement’s attempt to convince the states to join the settlement by creating a $280 million “settlement fund” for their state law claims was clearly insufficient to protect the public interest given the vast majority of states rejected the terms of the settlement and continued to litigate the case.10 And while other terms—such as the promise to implement a standardized 3 U.S. Dep’t of Justice, “Justice Department Will Move to Significantly Modify and Extend Consent Decree with Live Nation/Ticketmaster,” December 19, 2019, https://www.justice.gov/archives/opa/pr/justice-department-will-move-significantly-modify-and-extend-consent-decree-live.","score":0.41663104,"raw_content":null}],"response_time":0.74,"request_id":"019a5778-0f95-4e6a-a9de-d91472f90be8"}
